title: US Strikes Hit Iranian Bridges and Ports as Hormuz Standoff Deepens, IEA Warns on Energy Security, NATO Silence on Turkey Rights Draws Scrutiny
subhead: A sixth consecutive night of US airstrikes hit Bandar Abbas, Qeshm Island, and bridges in southern Iran, while Iran declared the Strait of Hormuz an 'unbreakable red line' and threatened regional infrastructure. The IEA warned that global energy security is at risk if Hormuz does not reopen within weeks. Meanwhile, Western silence on Turkey's democratic erosion deepens as Erdoğan's options narrow ahead of 2028.

sections.heading: US Strikes Expand to Bridges and Port Infrastructure as Conflict Enters Sixth Night
sections.body:
US Central Command carried out a sixth consecutive night of strikes on Iran on July 16, with CENTCOM stating the operations aimed to 'further degrade Iranian military capabilities.' For the first time in the current escalation, strikes hit Qeshm Island and multiple locations in Bandar Abbas, home to Iran's largest commercial port and key navy and Revolutionary Guard facilities, according to the semi-official Mehr news agency. Iranian state media also reported strikes on three bridges, a railway station in Bandar Khamir, and Iranshahr Airport in southeastern Iran, as well as explosions near Bushehr, where Iran's only civilian nuclear power plant is located.

The expansion to bridges and port infrastructure marks a significant escalation from earlier rounds that focused on military and coastal surveillance targets. President Trump has publicly threatened to strike Iranian power plants and bridges, and has not ruled out ground operations including seizing Kharg Island, Iran's main oil export terminal, Reuters reports.

Iran's military spokesperson Brigadier General Ebrahim Zolfaghari declared the Strait of Hormuz an 'unbreakable red line' and warned that Iran would target 'all infrastructure throughout the region' if Trump authorizes strikes on civilian or energy facilities, according to Gulf News and Iranian state media.
sections.whyItMatters: The shift from hitting military sites to destroying bridges and port infrastructure signals Washington is trying to degrade Iran's logistical capacity around Hormuz — not just its weapons. For Turkey, every escalation raises the stakes on energy costs, trade disruption, and the strategic value of overland corridors.

sections.heading: Hormuz Shipping Halted Again as Both Sides Enforce Blockades
sections.body:
Shipping through the Strait of Hormuz has largely halted again as both the US and Iran reimposed blockades this week. Iran resumed its blockade of the strait, while the US reinstated its naval blockade of Iranian ports on July 16, according to Reuters. Just 22 ships crossed the strait on July 9, compared to 147 the day before the war began in February, according to commodities tracker Kpler cited by NPR.

Iran has demanded all ships use a channel close to its shores and has said it intends to charge passage fees at the end of a 60-day negotiation period set in last month's memorandum of understanding. Trump initially announced a 20 percent US transit fee for Hormuz shipping on July 14, then reversed it on July 15 after pushback from Gulf Arab leaders, replacing it with a plan for Gulf states to make trade and investment deals with the US instead, NPR reports.

Oil prices rose for a fourth straight day on July 16, with Brent crude trading above $84 a barrel. Goldman Sachs warned that Chinese buying and depleted inventories could push prices to $110 per barrel if the disruption continues, according to Gulf Daily News.
sections.whyItMatters: The dual blockades — Iran asserting control, the US enforcing its own — create a shipping vacuum that pushes global oil prices higher and reinforces the strategic premium on Turkey's overland energy corridors. The fee structure confusion adds a layer of regulatory uncertainty that shipping companies will simply avoid.

sections.heading: IEA Warns Global Energy Security at Risk If Hormuz Does Not Reopen
sections.body:
International Energy Agency chief Fatih Birol warned that global energy security is at risk if the Strait of Hormuz does not reopen within weeks, Reuters reported on July 17. Birol, a Turkish economist who leads the IEA, said 'oil security is still a critical issue' and that the world should be 'worried' if the situation does not improve.

The warning came as the US revoked a waiver allowing Iran to sell oil internationally — undoing one of the few concessions from last month's interim deal — and as Iran signaled it could ask Houthi allies to close the Bab al-Mandeb strait at the mouth of the Red Sea if the US escalates further, three sources told Reuters. That would threaten an alternative route that some shipping had been using to bypass Hormuz.

Meanwhile, a drone struck a commercial ship near Iraq's southern port of Basra on July 16, according to Iraqi officials. The vessel had arrived from the UAE carrying American-branded vehicles. Separately, Iraq's Oil Ministry reported an unidentified object landing near an oil tanker, though with no fire or structural damage.
sections.whyItMatters: Birol's warning — from a Turk who understands the region's energy politics — underscores that the Hormuz crisis has moved from military confrontation to systemic energy risk. The Basra drone hit shows the conflict is already spilling into Iraq's oil infrastructure, directly relevant to Turkey's energy import calculus and the Kirkuk-Ceyhan pipeline.

sections.heading: Iran Retaliates Against Gulf States as Kuwait and Qatar Hit
sections.body:
Iran's retaliation for the US strikes has increasingly targeted US-allied Gulf states. Kuwaiti air defenses intercepted hostile missiles and drones early on July 17, with the Kuwait Army describing the attacks as 'criminal Iranian aggression,' Gulf News reports. Qatar's Ministry of Defence confirmed it repelled a missile attack, with a child injured by shrapnel from interception operations. Qatar earlier elevated its national security threat level to 'high,' with the Interior Ministry urging residents to stay indoors and avoid windows.

Iran also said it launched missiles and drones against US military infrastructure in Bahrain — home to the US Navy's 5th Fleet — and at US outposts in Jordan, according to NPR. Jordanian state media said its air defenses intercepted four Iranian missiles entering its airspace.

These retaliatory strikes on Gulf states are exactly the scenario that makes Turkey's position delicate: Ankara has deepened security ties with the UAE, Qatar, and Saudi Arabia in recent years, but remains officially neutral on the Iran conflict, calling for de-escalation while avoiding direct criticism of Tehran.
sections.whyItMatters: Iran's willingness to strike Kuwait, Qatar, and Bahrain — all US allies and all Turkey's regional partners — puts Ankara in an increasingly uncomfortable position. Every Gulf state hit is a Turkey partner hit, and the longer the conflict persists, the harder it becomes for Ankara to maintain its balanced posture.

sections.heading: NATO Allies Grow Silent on Turkey's Rights Erosion as Strategic Value Rises
sections.body:
A Reuters analysis published July 1 documented how Western governments have largely stopped publicly raising concerns about Turkey's democratic backsliding, prioritizing security ties with the regional military power instead. Five years ago, 10 ambassadors risked a diplomatic crisis by calling for a political prisoner's release; today, such statements have all but disappeared.

The shift accelerated after NATO's Ankara summit on July 8-9, where President Trump announced the lifting of CAATSA sanctions on Turkey and signaled willingness to sell F-35 jets. NATO Secretary General Mark Rutte said tens of billions of dollars in deals would be announced. Rights groups, including Human Rights Watch, documented a crackdown ahead of the summit: more than 200 arrests in Ankara, a ban on demonstrations, the jailing of comedian Deniz Göktaş for calling Erdoğan a dictator, and the blocking of an LGBTQ+ cruise ship from docking in Aydın, The Guardian reported on July 6.

Two journalists — T24 international editor Buse Söğütlü and OdaTV journalist Ceren Erdoğdu — were arrested on July 6. Ankara's prosecutor said the raids targeted 'terrorist organizations,' but HRW said no evidence was provided. Karol Wasilewski of the Center for Eastern Studies told Reuters that Western allies have 'given up on values to an extent and prefer a transactional relationship ... knowing that Turkey is indispensable for the defence of Europe.' He added that Ankara knows criticism 'won't translate into actions.'
sections.whyItMatters: The transactional logic is clear: Turkey is too valuable as a defense partner and drone exporter for the West to pressure on rights. But the silence has costs — it removes constraints on further crackdowns and reinforces the opposition's sense of abandonment. The Economist's analysis that Erdoğan is 'running out of tricks' noted his two remaining paths to extend power beyond 2028 require constitutional changes he lacks the votes for.

sections.heading: Markets Steady Despite War, but Lira Hits All-Time Low
sections.body:
The BIST 100 closed at 14,251.29 on July 16, up 1.22%, according to TradingEconomics, despite the Iran-Hormuz escalation. The index remains 37% higher than a year ago, though it has pulled back 1.2% over the past month from its all-time high of 15,204 in May.

The USD/TRY reached an all-time high of 47.17 on July 17, up 0.03% from the previous session, according to TradingEconomics. The lira has weakened 1.6% over the past month and 16.9% over the past year. Turkey's inflation stands at 32.1% (June 2026), with the central bank policy rate at 37%.

Brent crude traded at $84.69 a barrel on July 17, slipping slightly despite the expanded conflict — a sign that markets are pricing in continued disruption but not an imminent supply catastrophe. However, Goldman Sachs warned that sustained Hormuz closure and Chinese strategic buying could push prices to $110.
sections.whyItMatters: The lira at an all-time high against the dollar and inflation still above 32% show that Turkey's macro challenges persist regardless of the geopolitical noise. The BIST's resilience suggests investors are betting on Turkey's defense-sector growth and geopolitical leverage — but the lira's deterioration signals that fundamentals remain fragile.

sections.heading: Diplomacy Stalls as Iran and US Trade Interpretations of Failed Deal
sections.body:
The interim ceasefire memorandum signed last month between the US and Iran has all but collapsed, with both sides accusing the other of violating its terms. Iran's Deputy Foreign Minister Kazem Gharibabadi said the US had 'destroyed' the memorandum, Reuters reports. The core dispute centers on Paragraph 5 of the deal, which stipulates that Iran will make arrangements using its 'best efforts for the safe passage of commercial vehicles' — a clause that Washington interprets as guaranteeing free passage, but Tehran reads as acknowledging Iran's authority over the strait, NPR and the International Crisis Group reported.

The International Crisis Group called the situation 'another battlefield, with both sides using force to try to impose their own reading of the agreement's ambiguous terms.' Iran's parliament speaker and US negotiator Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf posted an image of the deal highlighting the 'Iran will make arrangements' language, writing: 'The era of one-sided deals is OVER.'

Despite the fighting, both sides have left diplomatic channels open. Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said his government was in touch with mediators including Oman, Qatar, and Pakistan. White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt said Trump 'is always open to diplomacy.' Three US officials told Reuters the current strikes could serve as 'shaping operations' giving Trump more options before potential bigger steps.
sections.whyItMatters: The ambiguous language of the interim deal — designed to paper over disagreements — is now the fuel for renewed conflict. Both sides claim the other violated the deal first, and neither can afford to appear weak. For Turkey, which has positioned itself as a potential mediator, the failure of the memorandum removes the only diplomatic framework that existed.

bottomLine: The US-Iran conflict escalated sharply on July 16-17, with strikes hitting Iranian bridges, port infrastructure, and civilian areas for the first time, while Iran retaliated against Kuwait, Qatar, and Bahrain. The IEA warned that global energy security is at risk if Hormuz does not reopen soon. For Turkey, the strategic calculus is tightening: the lira hit an all-time low, Gulf partners are under Iranian fire, and Western governments — fresh from praising Turkey at the NATO summit — are conspicuously silent on its domestic rights erosion. The failed memorandum's collapse leaves no diplomatic framework in place, and both sides are expanding target sets. Ankara's careful balancing act between Western alliance and regional neutrality is getting harder to maintain with every passing night of strikes.
sources: Reuters, NPR, Gulf News, Kathmandu Post/Reuters, Guardian, Time, Yahoo News/Reuters, USNews/Reuters, TradingEconomics, Goldman Sachs via Gulf Daily News, Human Rights Watch, IEA via MSN/Reuters, CENTCOM, Iran state media (Mehr, IRIB), Kuwait Army, Qatar Ministry of Defence, International Crisis Group
